analogy: Think of "Motion" through the chapter's own opening images: Journey Inside the Atom Chapter 8 Everything that you see or observe, or feel around is matter. You have learnt that matter consists of tiny particles called atoms. Take a closer look at the picture given above. Do you notice that both living beings, like... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Motion is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that journey Inside the Atom Chapter 8 Everything that you see or observe, or feel around is matter.

The text explains that you have learnt that matter consists of tiny particles called atoms.

The text explains that take a closer look at the picture given above.

The text explains that do you notice that both living beings, like us, and non-living things, like a house, are ultimately composed of atoms?

The text explains that these atoms are so tiny that they cannot be seen with the naked eye.

The text explains that you may be wondering — Is an atom truly the smallest unit of matter, or can it be divided even further?

The text explains that scientists, too, have been exploring whether atoms are divisible.

The text explains that if so, what are their constituents and how are these arranged?

The text explains that let us examine how the concept of atoms emerged and how it has been evolved since then.

The text explains that grade 8 Curiosity Chapter 7 Atoms Si N O H P C Human Body Organ Tissue Cell Silicates Brick Wall Room House Protein Cell Think It Over y Are atoms the smallest indivisible particles? y Why do electrons not fall into the nucleus even though they are attracted to protons in it? y Why did scientists keep modifying atomic models?

The text explains that 141 Journey I nside the Atom 8.1 Rediscovering the Roots of Atomic Theory Let us embark on a journey that takes us back more than 2,000 years, to the intellectual landscapes of ancient India and ancient Greece.

The text explains that in these distant yet remarkably parallel civilisations, profound thinkers, such as Acharya Kanada in India, and Leucippus and Democritus in Greece, pondered over the same fundamental question that has continued to inspire human inquiry across centuries — What is everything made up of?

The text explains that acharya Kanada suggested that if matter (dravya) is divided repeatedly, you will reach a stage where you would encounter the smallest particles that can no longer be divided.

The text explains that his ideas are recorded in the Sanskrit text Vaisesika Sutras.

The text explains that a parmanu is infinitely small and cannot be perceived by the senses.

The text explains that combinations of these forms dyads (groups of two parmanus) and triads (groups of three parmanus), and so on.

The text explains that thomson studied the conduction of electric current through gases at a very low pressure.

The text explains that as you have learnt in Chapter 4, Describing Motion Around Us, a particle moving in a circular path is constantly changing direction, which means it is accelerating.

The text explains that aman was discussing the structure of atom with his classmates.

The text explains that during the discussion, he learnt that an element X has a mass number of 35 and contains 18 neutrons.

The text explains that (vi) What will be the mass number if two neutrons are added to its nucleus?

The text explains that (vii) What will be the relation of X with the new atom?

The text explains that in an atom, there are 12 protons and 12 neutrons in the nucleus.

The text explains that now, imagine that all the electrons are replaced with some hypothetical particles that have the same charge as electrons but are 500 times heavier.

The text explains that what effect will this replacement have on the atom’s: (i) Atomic number (ii) Atomic mass (iii) Mass number (iv) Overall charge

The text explains that 161 Journey I nside t he A t om y Create an ‘Atomic Prediction Board’ game based on atomic number, mass number, number of electrons, protons, neutrons and valency.

The text explains that students may predict elements using atomic clues. y Prepare a report on how the properties of atoms impact us in everyday life across fields, such as healthcare, energy, agriculture and technology. y Create a role-play, stage play or story about the ‘Journey Inside the Atom’, and the scientists who discovered and contributed to the identification of atomic structure. y Use selected software or digital tools and try to create animations or simulations of various atomic models, and share them in the class. y Watch a film or documentary about the structure of the atom and write a report answering the following questions:  Which film or documentary did you watch, and what was its main idea or topic?  What did the film or documentary teach you about the structure of the atom and the atomic model(s)?  Which scientists were mentioned in the film or documentary, and what were their contributions?  What part of the film or documentary did you find most interesting, and what question do you still have? y Draw a bar graph showing the number of electrons in each energy level for any three elements. y To learn more about atoms, you can explore the links given below:  https://phet.colorado.edu/en/simulations/rutherford-scattering  https://phet.colorado.edu/en/simulations/isotopes-and-atomic-mass The Quest Continues ...

The text explains that is it possible to completely understand everything that happens inside an atom?

Source: NCERT Class 9 Science, Chapter 8: Motion

prelims_traps: Do not treat 141 Journey I nside the Atom and 8.1 Rediscovering the Roots of Atomic Theory as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Motion; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Motion through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 141 Journey I nside the Atom and 8.1 Rediscovering the Roots of Atomic Theory to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
